title: Gold: overnight returns significantly positive, day-session returns significantly negative (30-year sample)
claim: Across COMEX front gold futures, London Fix spot, gold miners and gold ETFs, overnight (non-US-day) returns are significantly positive while day-session returns are significantly negative, over roughly 30 years and in both up and down gold markets — i.e., the Asia/overnight period systematically gains and the London/NY day session systematically bleeds.

source.label: 'Overnight versus day returns in gold and gold related assets', Journal of Economics and Finance (2017), Springer
source.quote: Overnight returns are significantly positive while day returns are significantly negative

rule_formal: r_overnight = close_prev_day_session -> open_next_day_session; r_day = open -> close of day session. E[r_overnight] > 0, E[r_day] < 0, both significant; asymmetry present in bull and bear subperiods and 'economically important even with transaction costs' (per abstract summary).

numbers.effect: Sign asymmetry only from abstract (positive overnight vs negative day); magnitudes are behind paywall
numbers.sample: ~30-year period; COMEX gold front futures, London Fix spot, gold mining stocks, gold closed-end funds and ETFs
numbers.caveats: Abstract-level access — effect sizes not extractable; 'day' is defined by US/COMEX session, which maps only partially onto our 08:00-20:00 server breakout window; sample ends ~2016, overlap with losing years 2017-2019 untested in this paper

mechanism: Authors frame it as gold being '(too) high at the opening' of day markets. Candidate forced-flow reading (flagged as interpretation, not from the paper): commercial supply (producer hedging, fix-related selling) concentrates in London/NY business hours while Asian physical demand accumulates overnight — consistent with the PM-fix downward drift claim (micro-lbma-pmfix-downward-drift) but the paper itself does not identify who is forced.
relevance: Q4/Q2: implies a structural long-bias overnight/Asia and short-bias during the day session — directly testable against our long vs short breakout PnL split by session and by era (did the day-session bleed weaken after 2015 fix reform / 2017-2019?)

test_design.lookahead_risk: Session boundaries are clock-based, known ex ante — a 'day-session short bias' prior needs no lookahead; but as an unconditional drift it is a weak per-trade signal

overlap_risk: Day/overnight split partially overlaps gap (overnight return IS a gap-family variable — gap was tested dead at day level, so any value must come from session-conditional direction, not gap magnitude); orthogonal to rv/slope/range_exp/aw
